questions.question: An attacker obtains a valid SAML assertion and replays it within the validity window. Which weakness is most likely involved?
options: Insufficient assertion audience or replay protection
questions.description: SAML assertions should be audience-bound, time-limited, signed, and protected from replay with appropriate validation.

questions.question: Which detection is most precise for credential dumping from LSASS on Windows?
options: Unexpected process access to LSASS memory
questions.description: Credential dumping commonly requires suspicious handle access or memory reads against the LSASS process.

questions.question: Which zero trust principle is most directly violated by broad network access after VPN login?
questions.description: Zero trust requires continuous verification and narrowly scoped access rather than network-wide trust.

questions.question: What is the strongest reason to separate detection logic from alert routing rules?
options: Reusable detections can feed multiple response paths
questions.description: Decoupling detection logic from routing keeps detections reusable while routing adapts to teams, severity, and workflows.

questions.question: Which action most improves incident containment speed for compromised cloud keys?
questions.description: Automated revocation and scoped credentials reduce attacker dwell time after key exposure.
